The basic disqualification provision of the Act is contained

No public official at any level uf state or local

January 20, 1978
Fair: Political Practices Commission
926 "J" Street, Suite 522
Sacramento, California
95514
Gentlemen:
I hereby respectfully request written advice from the Fair
Political Practices Commission for guidance in my official
activities bascd on the following facts which 1 believe are
material:
MATERIAL FACTS
a member nf my immediate family wishes to convey to no, as a
gift, a parcel of real property immediately adjacent to the
University Redevelopment Project area in the old Quad area of
Santa Clara,
The property is now, and has since 1941, served as
a family home, and consists of s Victorian-style house built at
or near the turn of the century, but has long been located in a
co (Commercial>Downtown) zoning district.
No current appraisal
of the property has been done, and because of the unique
architecture and location of the house, the current market value
is unknown.
The Redevelopment Project has been certified as substantially
complete by the Redevelopment Agency.
The remaining undeveloped
portion of the area, being slightly over 7 acres, will be sold by the
City of Santa Clara as surplus property, subject to the requirements
of the Redevelopment Plan.
Proposals for development of this land
will be presented to the City Council, which will then decide to
which developer the land will be soldr
Pursuant to the Redevelop-
ment Plan, the design and construction of the development are
subject to the approval of the city Council,
Even if no present gift is made, there exists a strong likelihood
that 1 will eventually succeed to ownership of the property by
will or intestate succession.
REQUESTED OPINION
In accordancc with Covctnrent Code a ssllmb) as implemented by your
regulation 123329, I respectfully request that the Fair Political
Practices commission provide written advice with respect to my


,

'
Conflict of interest duties in connection with City Council
consideration of proposals for final development of the Redevelopment
Project Area.
lf there are any additional facts or other material
you might desire, please contact me so 1 can make them available
to you.
I would also appreciate your expeditious handling of this
request so that I will have the beneiit of the decision for use
with regard to the proposals.
Very truly yours,

sections.question: s, please do not hesitate to contact me.
sections.conclusion: The basic disqualification provision requires: (1) an interest in real property; (2) foreseeability that the decision will affect that interest; (3) materiality of the effect; and (4) distinguishability from effects on the public generally. A mere expectancy from a contemplated gift or inheritance is insufficient to create an interest requiring disqualification. If the residence would be affected in substantially the same manner as other residences in the vicinity, disqualification would not be required under Commission Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.
sections.question_synthetic: A Santa Clara city councilman seeks guidance on his conflict of interest obligations regarding participation in city council decisions about a redevelopment project area, given that he expects to receive a family property adjacent to the project area either as a gift or through inheritance.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The councilman is not disqualified based on a mere expectancy of receiving the property through gift or inheritance, as such future interests do not constitute sufficient financial interests under the Political Reform Act. Disqualification would only apply if the redevelopment decisions would materially and distinctly affect his property differently from other residential properties in the area.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (dated January 20, 1978) and FPPC response letter (dated February 27, 1978). Minor OCR errors present but content is generally clear. Date discrepancy noted: response header shows February 27, 1972 but document ID and context indicate 1978. Extracted FPPC response sections per instructions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: s, please do not hesitate to contact me.

CONCLUSION: The basic disqualification provision requires: (1) an interest in real property; (2) foreseeability that the decision will affect that interest; (3) materiality of the effect; and (4) distinguishability from effects on the public generally. A mere expectancy from a contemplated gift or inheritance is insufficient to create an interest requiring disqualification. If the residence would be affected in substantially the same manner as other residences in the vicinity, disqualification would not be required under Commission Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Councilman John Mahan of Santa Clara that he is not disqualified from participating in redevelopment project decisions based on his expected inheritance or gift of adjacent property, since mere expectancies do not constitute financial interests under the Political Reform Act, and any effects would likely be felt by all neighboring homeowners similarly.
